text: So this would seem a rather straightforward laparoscopic high anterior resection for a locally advanced distal sigmoid proximal rectal cancer. But what made this case a bit more challenging are the adhesions from his previous laparotomy. So At the start of the operation we were standing on the left side of the patient taking down all these adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall to facilitate safe entry of the rest of the ports. Another challenging aspect about this case really is that this guy has a long and loopy sigmoid colon as you can see in the CAT scan. that's fairly stuck to the right side. This has to be straightened up before you can actually begin with the standard steps of the operation.
The surgeon uses monopolar cautery to carefully dissect and release adhesions in the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon moves to the right side of the patient to begin the neutral to lateral resection, opening the peritoneum widely. The goal is to locate the inferior mesenteric pedicle package, with attention to the duodenum marking the cephalic extent of the dissection. A sizable lymph node in the mesenteric pedicle is identified for en bloc removal.

The surgeon exposes the smooth and shiny surface of the inferior mesenteric pedicle package, proceeding with dissection behind this structure. The dissection advances towards the root of the inferior mesenteric artery, where the surgeon prepares to skeletonize the artery for the application of hemo locks.
The surgeon identifies a sizable lymph node within the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) chain and prepares to excise it with appropriate margins.

The surgeon completes the skeletonization of the inferior mesenteric artery down to the adventitia level. Hemolocks are applied to secure the vessel, which is then transected at a high point. The cut end of the mesenteric pedicle is grasped to facilitate further medial to lateral dissection.

The surgeon begins pelvic dissection, identifying the inferior hypogastric nerve in the surgical field. The dissection follows the delicate nerve fibers, known as 'angel's hair,' extending into the deep pelvis with the aim of preserving the nerve.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection, carefully following the areolar tissues and pushing the visceretum upwards. A clear dissection plane is exposed by cutting on the yellow side of the white tissue. The surgeon employs a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques, ensuring the vertebral structures remain in their correct position.

The surgeon continues dissection on the lateral side, removing adhesions to expose the left gutter up to the urinary bladder. The previously faced challenges appear manageable as most of the dissection has been completed.

The surgeon unzips the peritoneum in the pelvic region, ensuring the left ureter remains unharmed. Dissection proceeds close to the mesorectum to avoid nerves in the lateral sidewalls. The area near the tumor is dissected, revealing a portion of the urinary bladder. The dissection continues posteriorly and laterally, following areolar tissue planes.

The surgeon uses a colonoscope to accurately localize the tumor, then dissects distally to achieve a distal transection margin. The distal transection point is measured at five centimeters, and the tissue is cut perpendicularly across this point.

The surgeon focuses on the pyramus enteric vein, located just below the border of the pancreas, and begins skeletonizing it to ensure no other vessels are present. The mesentery is trimmed to facilitate extraction through the fan solid sessions.
The surgeon uses an open stapler to transect the distal margin in a single maneuver, preparing for the colorectal anastomosis. The procedure concludes with the application of the jacuzzi test.
The surgeon uses a colonoscope to visually inspect the anastomotic site, checking for integrity and ensuring there are no leaks or defects.
